title: $1b windfall props up Victoria surplus
full_text:
The Allan government has relied on a $1.1 billion windfall from Victoria's compulsory third-party vehicle insurance scheme - a $700 million improvement on a forecast from five months ago - to underpin the state's razor-thin operating surplus in the next financial year.
The mid-year budget update released in December projected the Transport Accident Commission, the statutory insurer of third-party personal liability for road accidents, would pay the government $408 million in 2026-27 and $8 million the following year.
But Tuesday's budget papers showed the government will now receive a $1.1 billion dividend from the TAC next financial year. That will collapse to just $8 million in 2027-28 before rising to $708 million and then $608 million over the remainder of the forward estimates, signalling the importance of the windfall in 2026-27 to propping up Victoria's operating surplus.
A government spokesman said the TAC's past and projected financial performance means it has capacity to pay these dividends to the state while maintaining a strong financial position.
"This budget invests more than $1 billion to repair and make our roads safer for Victorians," he said. "There has been no reduction to TAC's road safety expenditure over the past year."
Treasurer Jaclyn Symes' second budget attempted a high-stakes fiscal balancing act of delivering sweeteners for a November 28 state election while grappling with mounting debt and interest repayments.
It revealed a $900 million hole in the government's operating balance in 2026-27 as it failed to find enough savings to offset billions of dollars in new spending. Next year's operating surplus was slashed from $1.9 billion to $900 million despite a $4 billion uplift in revenue - a quarter of that from the TAC dividend alone.
Once the cost of capital infrastructure projects is considered, Victoria's budget bottom line shows continued deterioration. The state was forecast to post a $7.7 billion cash deficit in 2026-27, $400 million more than projected in December.
Net debt is on track to climb to $199.3 billion in mid-2030 and gross debt is projected to soar to $244 billion. Interest repayments are tipped to lift from $8.9 billion next financial year - 7.8 per cent of revenue - to $11.8 billion, or 9.56 per cent of revenue, at the end of the decade.
The government has been under scrutiny over its failure to meaningfully rein in spending, with global credit ratings agency S&P on Tuesday warning Victoria's precarious fiscal recovery had been prolonged by Labor's decision to spend any budget revenue gains rather than reduce debt.
The Department of Treasury and Finance projected its revenue from dividends, a portion of the corporations profits distributed to the Victorian government, and income tax equivalents was expected to be $2.2 billion in total in 2026-27 - up from $1.5 billion forecast in December - before moderating to $2 billion by 2029-30.
This is the third year the TAC has paid a dividend above $1 billion after then-treasurer Tim Pallas requested a $1.08 billion dividend in 2023-24 and Symes received $1.13 billion last year.
While Tuesday's budget papers do not disclose TAC's funding ratio, a measure of its financial health, the government believed its ratio last year was in the middle of its target range of 100 to 145 per cent. TAC's funding ratio was 161.7 per cent in 2020-21.
summary: Victoria's budget surplus for 2026-27 is significantly supported by a $1.1 billion dividend from the Transport Accident Commission, despite ongoing concerns about rising debt and spending.

heading: General Insurance
key_points: Victoria's operating surplus for 2026-27 is now projected at $900 million, down from $1.9 billion.
The TAC's dividend is crucial for the budget, contributing a quarter of the $4 billion revenue uplift.
Victoria's net debt is expected to reach $199.3 billion by mid-2030, raising concerns about fiscal sustainability.
relevance: This information is relevant for actuaries and financial professionals as it highlights the impact of insurance dividends on state budgets and the implications for fiscal health and debt management.
